skill.name: radix-ui-design-system
skill.description: Build accessible React design systems with Radix UI primitives, compound component patterns, theming guidance, and production component examples.
skill.summary: Create accessible, customizable Radix UI component systems for React applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical alerts, but manual review found they are false positives caused by Markdown, JSX examples, template placeholders, documentation links, and fixed package imports. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, command injection, or malicious persistence was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static heuristic false positive from combined benign patterns
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The critical and high heuristic alerts combine unrelated documentation snippets, URLs, and examples. The reviewed content does not show credential access, network exfiltration, or executable code paths controlled by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged material is visible documentation and resource links. No code path sends secrets, reads credentials, or performs automatic network requests.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are text and UI false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts point to words such as design-system text, Markdown extensions, Radix descriptions, and CSS variant names. No cryptographic function or weak hashing algorithm is used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection found prose, JSX descriptions, and template labels at the cited lines. There is no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related cryptographic API call.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples misclassified as external command execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command findings come from fenced code examples and inline filenames in documentation. The skill does not execute Ruby backticks, shell backticks, child processes, or user-controlled commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown headings, import examples, and npm or npx install instructions. They are user-visible documentation, not code executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Relative documentation links misclassified as path traversal
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The filesystem alerts are relative Markdown links from the examples README to files in the same skill package. They do not read files dynamically or accept user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only evidence is static Markdown links using ../ to reference SKILL.md and a template file. No filesystem API is present at those locations.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs are benign resource links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URL findings are links to Radix UI, shadcn/ui, CVA, Discord, and GitHub resources. They are not used for telemetry, downloads, or exfiltration by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in resource sections and Markdown links. No fetch, curl, webhook, or network client code accompanies them.

low_findings.title: Dynamic imports use fixed Radix package names
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The dynamic import findings are React lazy-loading examples with hardcoded Radix UI package names. There is no user input, remote module URL, or runtime code generation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports target fixed npm package specifiers in a performance optimization example. This is a normal React code-splitting pattern.

low_findings.title: Certificate and reconnaissance alerts are keyword collisions
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The sensitive key alert points to keyboard shortcut handling, and the reconnaissance alert points to the asChild component guidance. No certificate files, private keys, or system reconnaissance commands were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain UI composition guidance and KeyboardEvent key checks. They do not access secrets, certificates, hostnames, processes, or system metadata.

content.value_statement: Teams need accessible React components without losing visual control. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Radix UI patterns, theming, and reusable component architecture.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Radix UI primitives for accessible React components.
Shows compound component patterns for Dialog, Dropdown, Tabs, Popover, Select, and Tooltip.
Provides theming guidance using CSS variables, Tailwind, and class variance patterns.
Includes reusable templates for Radix-based component wrappers.
Documents accessibility checks for focus, ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, and contrast.
Offers troubleshooting advice for common Radix integration issues.
limitations: It targets React projects and does not provide full implementations for other frameworks.
It is a guidance skill, not a complete packaged design system.
Examples require adaptation to match each project styling stack.
It does not replace manual accessibility testing with assistive technologies.

use_cases.title: Create accessible product components
use_cases.description: Use Radix primitives to build dialogs, menus, tabs, and forms with keyboard and screen reader support.

use_cases.title: Standardize a design system
use_cases.description: Define consistent component APIs, variants, CSS variables, and reusable wrapper patterns for a React UI library.

use_cases.title: Review component accessibility
use_cases.description: Check Radix-based components for focus management, ARIA labeling, contrast, and controlled state issues.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Radix component
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create an accessible Radix UI Dialog component for a React app. Include structure, required accessibility parts, and styling hooks.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt an existing component
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a component already in a codebase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React component and refactor it to use Radix UI primitives. Preserve the public API and explain any accessibility changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a component system
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a reusable design system architecture.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Radix UI component architecture for dialogs, dropdowns, tooltips, and tabs. Include variants, theming tokens, and composition rules.

prompt_templates.title: Audit advanced interactions
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these Radix-based components for focus management, controlled state, portal behavior, keyboard navigation, and WCAG risks.

output_examples.input: Create a modal pattern for editing a profile.
output_examples.output: A structured Dialog plan with trigger, portal, overlay, title, description, form fields, close controls, and focus management notes.

output_examples.input: Plan a reusable dropdown menu component.
output_examples.output: A component breakdown covering root, trigger, content, items, separators, checkbox items, radio groups, submenus, and keyboard behavior.

output_examples.input: Review a Radix design system for production readiness.

best_practices: Keep Radix accessibility primitives intact and style around them.
Use stable component APIs with explicit controlled and uncontrolled state patterns.
Test keyboard navigation, focus restoration, screen reader labels, and contrast before release.
anti_patterns: Do not remove required title, description, or label elements to simplify markup.
Do not mix controlled and uncontrolled state in the same component instance.
Do not override Radix keyboard or focus behavior without a tested accessibility reason.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build accessible React component systems with Radix UI primitives and custom styling.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made visual components?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on patterns, templates, and guidance for creating your own components.

faq.question: Can I use it with Tailwind CSS?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance includes Tailwind-friendly styling patterns and class composition approaches.

faq.question: Is it useful for shadcn/ui projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains raw Radix patterns that can help customize or extend shadcn/ui components.

faq.question: Does Radix UI guarantee accessibility?
faq.answer: Radix provides strong accessible primitives, but your content, labels, contrast, and tests still matter.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
